Hello everybody and welcome to the first installment of seven figure emails. This one is called the user agreement. And if this is the first time you're joining us for one of these sessions, this is how it's going to go down. We're first going to give a little bit of a background on the template, how it came to be. Then we're going to dive into some of the persuasion principles to really see what's at play, why we're using this, why it works and what to keep in mind when you write it yourself. Then we're going to look at the template itself. Finally, in the next video, we're going to look at an example so you can see how it's actually played out in a successful launch before. So that's what's going on. Here is the background. So this all came to be when I was writing actual post purchase user agreements for a lot of the online course creators that I was serving. So this wasn't something that was going into the launch materials initially. This was something that buyers would be getting in the onboarding sequence after they've already enrolled. I noticed while I was writing it that what I was actually writing, I was describing what would make the perfect student or the perfect buyer, what were the qualities, what were the things they'd be doing that would lend themselves to them actually getting stellar results in the program. I thought to myself, why not actually try to take this and put this in the launch sequence? The whole purpose of coaching the conversion is to coach the transformation between point A and point buyer. What's implicit in that is actually having someone identify as a buyer, why not put a document that actually reflects what a perfect buyer is within the launch itself. It all started off as an experiment. We did it. We took the actual user agreement. We put it in the closing email sequence instead, well, not instead in addition to the post purchase. We essentially said, if you sign up, this is what you're going to be getting immediately after you enroll. You might as well see right now, this is something that you would agree to. That was it. It was just taking the user agreement and then putting it into the sale sequence. Turns out that the experiment was a success. It was one of the heavy hitting launch emails in the sequence. The launch did extremely well and a template was born out of it. That's how it came to be. Just as a note, if you don't currently have a post purchase user agreement, this template actually is an excellent way to write that as well in a way that could have your prospect. Reaffirm the decision. Know what's required of them, how they're implicit in the process of getting the results you promised, and then of course, reminding them just of the value that you're here to provide them and coach against buyers remorse. All right. All that said, here's what's at play here. The first thing, like many of the templates across Tenix launches, if you're enrolled in that, six-figure emails, if you've taken some of the lessons and templates from that one, the user agreement stands out because it uses a framing device that your prospect hasn't seen before. We are taking a lot of the same things we'd be saying in a typical sales email, but we're delivering it in a way that is unique, fresh, and an interruption to what they're currently seeing. It's a pattern interrupt ad. It's core. We're not getting their attention by screaming or using cap slots or littering our email with gifts or whatever, but we are using a new framing device that they haven't really seen before and delivering really impactful information within it. Pattern interrupt is persuasion principle number one. Next it empowers the sophisticated buyer. The quickest way to overcome a sensitive BS meter is to really just be fully transparent around what's needed on your prospect's end to achieve the promised result with your product or offer or service as the tool. A lot of savvy buyers in 2020 have a BS radar for anything that promises quick solution, anything that is too good to be true, anything that is a magic pill. Yes, they want to believe that our product is going to powerfully solve their problems, but they don't want to feel like they've been taken out of the equation. They feel like they are implicit in it and there are things they're empowered to do with your product as the main tool. They still get to remain the hero. You're not coming off as someone who is over promising and triggering their BS defenses and that's a way that just creates more of a balanced equation for the buyer. That's what we're doing here. We are implying or we're making our buyer or our prospect implicit in the process of getting the result and we're building trust and credibility by doing it as well as excitement because they want to feel like they're getting credit. They want to feel like they're the hero of solving their problem. That's what's going on here. They want to feel implicated. We're giving them a chance to do that through what we're saying in the user agreement. Cool. Persuasion principle number three, it helps your prospect self-identify as a buyer. We spoke about this a little bit in the intro, but it bears repeating. We are coaching identification as a buyer. That is the main thing we're trying to do in our closing emails. We want our reader to identify as a buyer and by treating and communicating with your prospect as a buyer because this is literally what comes into buyer onboarding sequence. They get to actually experience the post purchase state ahead of time. Put another way, we're actually future pacing the immediate ownership experience. We're telling them exactly what they'll be getting if they buy, if they hit that enroll button, whatever they're doing. This is what's coming next. We're already prepaving what it's like to be a buyer and we're giving them a chance to raise our hand and say, yep, these are all things I would agree on, which brings us to the next one, which is the mid-crow commitment. I don't really like this word. I can think of anything catchier than this, but what I'm trying to communicate here is there's this common marketing or sales technique called micro commitment. Some people call trial closes. Essentially, you're trying to get your prospect to say a sequence of yeses or agree to a lot more smaller things before the final big offer. All of this exists in an ethical gray zone because you're essentially just training them in Pavlovian style to say yes, even if the things they're saying yes to are so far disconnected from the actual product. What we're doing here is we're actually getting those micro commitments or we're getting those yeses, but in a way that empowers them to get the results of our product. We're getting them to say, yes, I agree to do this. Yes, I agree to join the live coaching calls. I agree to set aside an hour every second or third day to go through the modules. Yes, I'm agreeing to do the work. We're actually getting them to agree micro commitment style to things that stack the deck in their favor of getting a result, which is much more ethical, much more empowered, and still getting the profound effect of having them say yes and really agree to the behaviors of the buyer. Cool. So persuasion principle number five, accurate and ethical if then logic. So if then logic is another powerful persuasion tool, if this, then you're a fit. In our case, we are using a very in depth and thorough if then logic. We're giving them the actual criteria that a buyer would have to sign. We're saying if you felt comfortable, if you felt empowered, if you felt good, agreeing to these terms as a buyer, then you belong in this program. So it's a lot more accurate. It's a lot more ethical than just getting someone to agree to a certain logic that is so disconnected from them actually getting results. So all in all, what we're doing is we're knocking down a smaller, but still a decent size domino. They still have to read this. They still have to agree that this is something that they can do and they're willing to do and that they're excited to do. And then knocking over that smaller domino, we're going to knock over the bigger one, which is them joining the program. Cool. Deep breath. All right. So what are we coaching? At its core, we're coaching self identification with a buyer by future pacing the very first step in the ownership experience. We're coaching consistency via a very powerful trial close, a mid-cro commitment. They're saying yes to the things that a successful buyer would. We're coaching them to remain consistent with that. And then of course, social proof and exclusivity. They're getting a document that only other buyers are getting. And it just creates a certain exclusive vibe. We're not talking to them as a prospect. We're talking to them with a certain level of respect like this is what our buyers get. This is what it looks like inside the club. Here's a chance to actually be in the club if you agree to the terms of the club. So that's what's going on template itself. Here's where you want to send it. You could send this as a standalone sales email as a one off. If you are doing it in an actual launch, I would put this earlier in the sales follow up after the conversion event, whether that conversion event is a webinar, a live launch announcement email, a presentation, a video series, whatever it is, I would put this as one of the earlier sales follow up emails to just really kind of show them that people are already enrolling. This is what you're sending people who have already joined the launch. This email doesn't really work as well as an urgency type email. So that's why I put it earlier on and then get a little shorter and really leverage more of the urgency triggers closer to a deadline. Cool. So here is what we would do. So first we're going to introduce the agreements. You'll see what that looks like. Then we're going to pre frame the agreement clauses as necessary for achieving the promised outcome. So these clauses aren't here just because they're arbitrary and we want to have them. This is actually what is necessary for them to get that outcome. And this is what builds stress, right? It's not a magic pill solution. These are here because it's necessary for them to get that result. And then clause one, you could have this related to them leveraging your USP. To overcome their biggest objection. Clause two point to another feature that overcomes the next biggest objection. So you're using these clauses also as sales tools, right? Yes, they are necessary, but they are also tied to overcoming objections and leveraging your USP. Clause three, I like this to be leveraging a point of exclusive empowerment. So something about them that gives them a chance to leverage a certain skill, leverage a certain trade, leverage a certain opportunity that they already have. You could also use this as a chance to highlight bonuses, right? Exclusive empowerment can mean if you join in the first 24 hours of this launch, you get a certain bonus, right? And this clauses, you agree to actually use this bonus to accelerate your results or get more results on top of it, whatever it looks like. Clause four specific actions they must do to get the result. Once again, here's a great time or a great way to future base. The actions are going to be doing in very specific detail and really get them starting to imagine themselves in the post purchase state. Clause five, celebrate them. Just find a way to honor them for the decision they've made. Because remember, this is really framed as something in the post purchase state. So celebrate the customer and then the call to action is if they agree. This is a really important one. So in the email version of this, if they could agree to these terms, then they're a perfect buyer, right? Because these are the clauses that we'd be sending them anyway and they'd be needing to agree to. So if they could agree to these, this is the commitment they're making, then they're a perfect buyer. And then, of course, we want to keep things totally balanced in the relationship. So we're going to close in the PS with three promises we're making in return. So the first promise could be a promise that positions you and gains competitors, how you're doing things differently, how you vow and promise to do things differently. A second promise could be to reinforce the exclusivity in clause three. So if you did promise that, if you did say in clause three that they'd be getting a certain bonus and being able to put that to use, your promise here is essentially saying no one else is going to be getting this, right? That we won't just let anyone have this bonus because we promised that only the first people or the first 10 people or the people in the first 24 hours could get access to it. Promise three could be a risk reversal and guarantee. So we promise to just stand by our guarantee that if things don't work out in 30 days, if you do the work and it's just not happening or if we don't deliver on this, then you could call us out on that and we will refund your money. And then to call to action once again, as if they agree. So that is the user agreement in a nutshell. In the next video below this, we're going to see it in action so you can really get a taste for what it looks like during an actual launch. So I'll see you in the next video.
